subject:
Re: Warp issues with multiple off-axis target collection naming
 convention
body:
Hi all,

We did some troubleshooting (and soul-searching) and we've managed to fix 
this issue, although the cause still seems unknown. We have added leading 
zeroes to the front of each tomogram so Position_2 becomes Position_002, 
Position_22 becomes Position_022, etc. From here, extracting from 
Position_22_2 no longer creates identical particles from 002 and 022. 
We've noticed this on another dataset which, like the above, exceeds 100 
tomograms (Position1 and Position101 are intertwined) so I would be 
cautious and check your outputted star files to ensure your picks are 
correctly extracted if working with large datasets.

Cheers,

Mat

On Monday, 16 January 2023 at 14:43:25 UTC emma.j....@gmail.com wrote:

> Dear All,
>
> Warp seems to being have issues with the naming convention arising from my 
> multiple off-axis target data collection using Tomo 5.11.
>
> In this collection, multiple tomograms could be collected per tracking 
> area. So if 4 tomograms are collected at a single position/tracking area 
> they would be named Position_1, Position_1_2, Position_1_3 and 
> Position_1_4, for example.
>
> This can result in tomograms with similar names e.g. Position_22, 
> Position_22_2 and Position_2.
>
> When I try to reconstruct subtomograms from my picked particles from this 
> dataset in Warp, the programme seems to get confused by similar names 
> arising from this convention, and repeats the extraction for Position_22 
> three times instead of extracting the particles picked from Position_22_2 
> and Position_2.
>
> i.e. 3 subtomo folders are created, but they all contain the same number 
> of particles, and identical particle co-ordinates, all for Position_22.
>
> We have tried to solve this issue by replacing underscores with zeroes in 
> the names, but we are still having the same problem.
>
> Does anyone have any suggestions on how we can get around this issue?
>
> Many thanks,
> Emma
>
>
>
